Docker layer caching: How do Docker laye: Each Dockerfile instruction creates a cached layer, and Docker reuses cached layers up to the first instruction whose input changed, so ordering matters - put rarely-changing steps (installing OS packages, dependency installs) before frequently-changing steps (copying application code) to maximize cache hits and speed up builds. For reproducibility, pin base image digests and dependency versions rather than floating tags, since a cache hit on a floating tag can silently produce a different image than a clean build.
Docker base images: How do you choose be: Ubuntu/Debian full images are easiest to debug (shell, package manager) but largest; slim variants trim unnecessary packages while keeping a shell; Alpine is much smaller but uses musl libc, which can cause subtle compatibility issues with some compiled dependencies (notably some Python/Node native modules); distroless has no shell or package manager at all, minimizing attack surface for production but making debugging harder. Choose distroless or Alpine for production runtime images once the build is stable, and keep a slim/full image available for local development and debugging.
Docker non-root containers: Why should c: Running as root inside a container means a container breakout or misconfigured volume mount can grant root-equivalent access on the host, and Kubernetes Pod Security Standards increasingly block root containers by default. Implement it with a `RUN useradd -u 1000 appuser` and `USER appuser` in the Dockerfile, ensure file permissions on copied files and writable directories are set for that UID, and set runAsNonRoot in the Kubernetes securityContext to enforce it at admission time.
Docker secrets: How do you prevent secre: Never COPY secret files into the image or pass them as build ARGs (they persist in image layer history); use BuildKit secret mounts (`RUN --mount=type=secret`) which are available only during that build step and never written to a layer. At runtime, inject secrets via a mounted volume from Secret Manager/Kubernetes Secrets rather than plain environment variables where possible, since env vars are visible via `docker inspect` and process listings, and scan images with a tool like trufflehog before pushing to catch accidental leaks.
Docker registry: How do you tag, push, s: Build one immutable image per commit tagged with the git SHA (not just `latest`), push it to Artifact Registry, and run vulnerability scanning automatically on push, blocking promotion on critical findings. Promote the exact same image digest through dev -> staging -> production by re-tagging or updating a deployment manifest, rather than rebuilding per environment, so what was tested is exactly what ships.
Docker troubleshooting: A container work: Start with `kubectl describe pod` and `kubectl logs` to see the exit reason and any startup errors, then check that all required environment variables/ConfigMaps/Secrets are actually mounted in the pod spec (a common gap between local `docker run` and a Kubernetes manifest). Verify the entrypoint command matches what Kubernetes expects (no interactive TTY assumptions), check filesystem permissions if the container runs as non-root or with a read-only root filesystem, and confirm readiness/liveness probe paths and ports match what the app actually exposes.
Docker networking: How does container ne: Locally, Docker creates a bridge network where containers get an internal IP and communicate via container name (Docker DNS) or published host ports; in Kubernetes, each pod gets its own IP on the cluster's pod network (via CNI), and Service objects provide a stable virtual IP and DNS name that load-balances across pod replicas, replacing Docker's single-host bridge model with a cluster-wide overlay/routed network plus kube-proxy or eBPF-based service routing.
Docker volumes: When would you use bind: Use bind mounts for local development to live-sync source code into a container; use named Docker volumes (or in Kubernetes, PersistentVolumes) for data that must survive container restarts and be managed independently of the container lifecycle, like database files; use ephemeral container storage (the writable layer or emptyDir in Kubernetes) for temporary scratch space that is fine to lose when the container restarts.
Docker Compose: When is Docker Compose u: Docker Compose is ideal for local development and simple single-host setups where you want to define and run a small set of interdependent containers (app, database, cache) with one command and minimal ceremony. Kubernetes is designed for multi-host production orchestration - scheduling, self-healing, rolling updates, service discovery across nodes, autoscaling - which Compose does not provide; teams typically use Compose for local dev/test and Kubernetes for staging/production.
Docker production readiness: What checks: The image should be built from a pinned, scanned base image with no critical/high vulnerabilities, run as a non-root user, have a minimal attack surface (no unnecessary packages or shells in distroless-style images), and define correct HEALTHCHECK or Kubernetes probe endpoints. It should also be built reproducibly from CI (not a developer laptop), signed/attested for provenance, and tagged immutably so the exact artifact tested in staging is the one deployed to production.
Scenario - registry outage during deploy: Pause the rollout rather than retrying blindly - Kubernetes will keep trying to pull the new image for pods it hasn't scheduled yet, which just produces ImagePullBackOff noise while the registry is down. Check whether the already-running new-version pods are healthy; if so, hold the mixed state (most services tolerate a brief mixed-version window) until the registry recovers, then resume the rollout, or roll back to the old image tag if the mixed state is causing user-facing issues and the old image is still cached/pullable. Afterward, add a registry mirror or pull-through cache so a single registry outage can't stall production deployments again.
Scenario - poisoned base image: A base i: Identify every image built from that base by scanning your registry/CI history for the base image digest, not just the tag, since a floating tag may have already moved. Rebuild and redeploy affected services from a patched base image, prioritizing internet-facing services first, and treat this as validation for pinning base images by digest rather than tag going forward so you always know exactly what's deployed. Add automated scanning on a schedule (not just at build time) so a newly-disclosed CVE in an already-deployed image is caught within hours instead of being discovered weeks later.
